title: 'No excuses' for hundreds of public servants off injured who can work
full_text:
Hundreds of public servants in NSW who are considered capable of working are being paid to stay on the sidelines due to psychological and physical injuries, prompting the state government to hunt for new roles they can perform.
NSW Industrial Relations Minister Sophie Cotsis says the number of public servants off injured but capable of working has halved, but hundreds are yet to return.
A return-to-work policy adopted by the Minns Labor government, which stresses the redeployment of injured workers, has, it says, slashed the number of public servants off work but capable of working by 56 per cent to 761.
According to the policy, if injured public servants had returned to work as soon as they were able, some $74.5 million could have been saved in 2023-24, the equivalent of 605 more teachers or 637 more nurses.
In 2023-24, there were a total of 3441 NSW public sector workers who at some point in the year had injuries, were assessed as having capacity to work, but for whom suitable work could not be found. More than half (55.5 per cent) had psychological injuries, and just less than half had primarily physical injuries. The policy blamed "physical or psychosocial obstacles" for preventing public servants from returning to their prior roles, suggesting that, in some cases, this could be solved by moving agencies.
The policy was released in April 2025 and made binding in December by Premier Chris Minns, a week before he secured Coalition support for a package of cuts to workers' compensation payments for psychological injury, which included more mentoring and support to return to work.
According to the government insurance agency icare's 2024-25 annual report, a sharp rise in claims for psychological injuries, including bullying, harassment and work pressure, helped drive the NSW workers' compensation scheme to losses of $1.7 billion in the private sector and $179 million in the public sector.
New figures provided to The Australian Financial Review reveal on June 30, 2024, about 1615 public servants were off work but had some capacity to work, a point-in-time figure less alarming than the 3441 who had capacity at any time during the year because some had already returned to work by June 30.
The number of workers off work due to injuries, but with capacity to work, increased to 1743 on June 30, 2025, before falling to 1183 in the September quarter, 911 in December, and 761 in March 2026.
"For too long, injured workers were being left to languish at home due to inefficiencies and a lack of co-ordination across government, despite having the capacity to return to work," Cotsis said.
She cited the example of a pathologist off work due to a psychological injury due to workplace bullying, who may be unable to return to their original place of work, but could work in another hospital, but was not called in to work because the second hospital was in a different local health district.
"The NSW government is one of the largest employers in the world, and prior to the Minns Labor government introducing this strategy, there had never been a genuine whole-of-government approach to tackling this issue. There are no more excuses, to me, as to why, other than misconduct or corruption, somebody who is serving our state ... who has the capacity to work [isn't working]. [If they have the capacity to work despite an injury] they shouldn't be languishing because we're not calling them."
Cotsis said the government provided public servants with case management and an individualised plan to return to work. "They've served the state, they've got amazing skills and qualifications. The government needs them back to work, to contribute ... and the workers want to contribute. It's the government's responsibility to find a pathway - can we look at retraining? Can we look at recognition of prior skills? And look at where we have vacancies that match those skills ... there should be no barrier."
Public Service Association assistant general secretary Troy Wright said the union supported redeployment during restructures to prevent public servants from being made redundant and in instances of people on workers' compensation who have capacity to work.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Karp
heading: General Insurance
